raw_consumer_statement:
Around XXXX 2026 Barclays Aviator AAdvantage credit card was charged approximately {$200.00} by a website advertising expedited passport services. The website presentation closely resembled an official passport application service and did not clearly disclose during the purchase process that it was a private third-party company charging a significant fee to provide documents that are otherwise freely available from the U.S. Department of State .

After completing the purchase, the company simply emailed or mailed standard passport application forms that can be downloaded directly from the U.S. Department of State website at no cost. No meaningful service was performed beyond sending publicly available documents. 

Because the service provided no legitimate value relative to the fee charged and the website presentation was misleading, I filed a billing dispute with Barclays requesting that the charge be reviewed. 

Barclays denied the dispute after the merchant stated that their terms indicate orders are non-refundable once documents are assembled or shipped. However, Barclays appears to have relied solely on the merchants stated policy rather than evaluating whether the merchants website presentation may be deceptive or whether the service provided reasonably justified the {$200.00} charge. 

My concern is that Barclays did not conduct a meaningful investigation of the dispute and instead accepted the merchants explanation at face value. As a card issuer, Barclays has an obligation to fairly investigate billing disputes and protect cardholders from misleading or deceptive merchant practices. 

I am requesting that Barclays conduct a proper review of this dispute and reconsider the charge.
